title: ProcessEndpoint.Terminate's escalation ladder can double-count closetime for slow scripts
body: In libwebsocketd/process_endpoint.go, Terminate() defines an escalation ladder (stdin close, SIGINT, SIGTERM, SIGKILL) where every step's timeout adds pe.closetime on top of a fixed base (100ms/250ms/500ms), so a large --closems value effectively gets applied three times sequentially before SIGKILL, making shutdown of a hung child process take far longer than the configured closetime alone would suggest.

title: createEnv's standardEnvCount comment-based tracking is a maintenance hazard
body: env.go's createEnv() precomputes a capacity hint via a hardcoded standardEnvCount = 20 (plus 1 for SSL) with a comment reminding contributors to update it whenever a new appendEnv call is added. It's easy to add a new CGI var and forget to bump the constant, which doesn't break correctness (append still grows the slice) but silently reintroduces reallocation on every request, defeating the purpose of the up-front capacity.

title: REMOTE_HOST reverse DNS lookup blocks the websocket handshake on slow DNS
body: createEnv() in env.go sets REMOTE_HOST from handler.RemoteInfo.Host, and per issue342_test.go / issue413_test.go this is populated via a reverse DNS lookup during connection setup. On networks with slow or unreachable reverse DNS this delays every single incoming connection's handshake, and there's no `--reverse-lookup=false`-style opt-out documented next to the Config in config.go.

title: Bash example scripts don't demonstrate handling SIGTERM for graceful cleanup
body: examples/bash/chat.sh, count.sh, and greeter.sh show basic websocketd usage patterns but none of them trap SIGTERM/SIGINT to flush buffered output or clean up temp files before the escalating termination ladder in process_endpoint.go reaches SIGKILL, which would be a useful pattern to document for real-world scripts.

title: bench/scenarios/connection_storm.js results aren't merged consistently with sustained_load.js runs
body: bench/lib/merge-runs.py is supposed to combine multiple benchmark run JSON outputs, but comparing bench/scenarios/connection_storm.js and sustained_load.js, the two scenarios emit differently shaped metrics objects (storm has burst-count fields, sustained_load has duration-based throughput fields), and merge-runs.py doesn't document which fields it expects to be common across scenario types before merging.
